rules.title: Creation of Agency
rules.prompt_question: How is an agency relationship created?
rules.rule_statement: Agency is the fiduciary relationship that arises when a principal manifests assent that an agent shall act on the principal's behalf and subject to the principal's control, and the agent manifests assent or otherwise consents so to act.
rules.application_example: A business owner hires a manager to enter contracts for the store subject to the owner's oversight.
rules.common_trap: Agency can exist even without a formal contract or payment if the elements of assent, benefit, and control are present.

rules.title: Types of Principals
rules.prompt_question: What are the types of principals in agency law?
rules.rule_statement: A principal may be disclosed, partially disclosed, or undisclosed. A disclosed principal is known to the third party and the third party knows the agent acts for a principal. A partially disclosed principal is known to exist but not identified. An undisclosed principal is not known to exist.
rules.application_example: An agent signs a contract without revealing the principal's identity, creating a partially disclosed principal situation.
rules.common_trap: The principal's disclosure status affects the liability of the principal and agent on contracts.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Express Actual Authority
rules.prompt_question: When does an agent have express actual authority?
rules.rule_statement: An agent has express actual authority when the principal expressly communicates to the agent authority to act on the principal's behalf.
rules.application_example: A company expressly tells its purchasing agent to buy 100 computers.
rules.common_trap: Express actual authority depends on communications from principal to agent, not from principal to third party.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Implied Actual Authority
rules.prompt_question: When does an agent have implied actual authority?
rules.rule_statement: An agent has implied actual authority to do acts reasonably necessary to accomplish an expressly authorized task or acts customarily associated with the agent's position.
rules.application_example: A store manager expressly authorized to run the store has implied authority to order routine supplies.
rules.common_trap: Implied actual authority is inferred from the principal's manifestations to the agent and surrounding circumstances.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Apparent Authority
rules.prompt_question: When does apparent authority arise?
rules.rule_statement: Apparent authority arises when a principal's manifestations to a third party would cause a reasonable third party to believe that the agent is authorized to act on the principal's behalf.
rules.application_example: A company gives an employee the title 'Vice President of Sales,' causing customers reasonably to believe the employee can enter ordinary sales contracts.
rules.common_trap: Apparent authority is based on the principal's manifestations to the third party, not the agent's self-serving statements.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Inherent Agency Power
rules.prompt_question: What is inherent agency power?
rules.rule_statement: Inherent agency power is an older doctrine under which a principal may be bound by certain acts of an agent that are usual or incidental to the agent's position, even absent actual authority, particularly to protect third parties dealing with an agent of an undisclosed principal.
rules.application_example: A manager of a business buys ordinary goods from a supplier, binding an undisclosed owner even if the owner privately restricted that power.
rules.common_trap: Modern analysis often prefers apparent authority or estoppel, but MEE outlines still list inherent agency power.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Agency by Estoppel
rules.prompt_question: When is a person estopped to deny agency?
rules.rule_statement: A person may be estopped to deny agency when the person intentionally or carelessly causes a third party to believe another has authority to act for the person, and the third party justifiably and detrimentally relies on that belief.
rules.application_example: A business allows someone to appear as its purchasing agent, and a vendor extends credit in reliance on that appearance.
rules.common_trap: Estoppel focuses on fairness and detrimental reliance, not true consent between principal and agent.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Ratification
rules.prompt_question: How does a principal ratify an unauthorized act?
rules.rule_statement: A principal ratifies an unauthorized act by affirming it after the fact or by accepting its benefits with knowledge of the material facts. Ratification retroactively creates the effect of authority if the principal had capacity and the act could have been authorized at the time.
rules.application_example: A principal learns an agent made an unauthorized purchase and keeps the goods, thereby ratifying the contract.
rules.common_trap: Ratification requires knowledge of material facts and cannot unfairly prejudice intervening third-party rights.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Agent Liability on Contract for Disclosed Principal
rules.prompt_question: When is an agent liable on a contract made for a disclosed principal?
rules.rule_statement: An agent who enters a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal is generally not liable on the contract if the agent had authority and the contract indicates representative capacity.
rules.application_example: An agent signs 'ABC Corp., by Jane Doe, Agent' and is not personally liable if ABC is disclosed and authorized.
rules.common_trap: An agent may still be liable if the agent lacked authority or expressly agreed to be bound.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Agent Liability for Undisclosed or Partially Disclosed Principal
rules.prompt_question: When is an agent liable on a contract for an undisclosed or partially disclosed principal?
rules.rule_statement: An agent who contracts on behalf of an undisclosed or partially disclosed principal is generally liable on the contract unless the parties agree otherwise.
rules.application_example: An agent buys goods without revealing the principal and is personally liable to the seller.
rules.common_trap: The third party may often elect to hold either the principal or the agent liable once the principal is discovered.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Principal Liability on Authorized Contracts
rules.prompt_question: When is a principal liable on a contract made by an agent?
rules.rule_statement: A principal is liable on a contract made by an agent acting with actual authority, apparent authority, inherent agency power where recognized, or ratified authority.
rules.application_example: An authorized sales agent signs a supply contract that binds the principal company.
rules.common_trap: If no authority or ratification exists, the principal is usually not bound unless estoppel applies.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Implied Warranty of Authority
rules.prompt_question: What liability does an agent face when acting without authority?
rules.rule_statement: An agent who purports to act for a principal impliedly warrants to the third party that the agent has authority. If the agent lacks authority and the principal is not bound, the agent may be liable for breach of that implied warranty of authority.
rules.application_example: An agent signs a lease for a company without authorization and may be liable to the landlord if the company is not bound.
rules.common_trap: The remedy is not always the contract price; it is usually damages for loss caused by the false representation of authority.

rules.title: Termination of Actual Authority
rules.prompt_question: How is actual authority terminated?
rules.rule_statement: Actual authority terminates by revocation by the principal, renunciation by the agent, occurrence of circumstances causing the agent reasonably to believe the principal no longer consents, completion of the task, lapse of time, or death or loss of capacity of the principal or agent.
rules.application_example: A principal revokes an agent's authority before the agent signs the contract.
rules.common_trap: Even after actual authority ends, apparent authority may linger as to third parties.

rules.title: Termination of Apparent Authority
rules.prompt_question: How is apparent authority terminated?
rules.rule_statement: Apparent authority terminates when it is no longer reasonable for the third party to believe the agent is authorized, usually after notice from the principal or other facts that make reliance unreasonable.
rules.application_example: A principal informs customers that a former employee no longer has authority to act for the business.
rules.common_trap: Termination of actual authority alone does not automatically end apparent authority as to third parties who have not received notice.

rules.title: Respondeat Superior
rules.prompt_question: When is a principal or employer vicariously liable for an agent's tort?
rules.rule_statement: An employer is vicariously liable for torts committed by an employee within the scope of employment under the doctrine of respondeat superior.
rules.application_example: A delivery driver negligently causes an accident while making deliveries for the employer.
rules.common_trap: The key issue is whether the tortfeasor is an employee rather than an independent contractor and whether the conduct was within the scope of employment.

rules.title: Scope of Employment
rules.prompt_question: How do courts determine whether conduct was within the scope of employment?
rules.rule_statement: Conduct is within the scope of employment if it is of the kind the employee was employed to perform, occurs substantially within authorized time and space limits, and is motivated at least in part by a purpose to serve the employer.
rules.application_example: A cashier negligently injures a customer while stocking merchandise during a work shift.
rules.common_trap: A substantial personal frolic may remove conduct from the scope, while a minor detour usually does not.

rules.title: Frolic and Detour
rules.prompt_question: What is the difference between a frolic and a detour?
rules.rule_statement: A detour is a minor deviation from assigned work that remains within the scope of employment, while a frolic is a substantial departure for personal purposes that takes the employee outside the scope of employment until the employee returns to the employer's business.
rules.application_example: A driver briefly stops for coffee while on a delivery route, which is likely a detour rather than a frolic.
rules.common_trap: Once the employee resumes the employer's work, scope of employment may be restored.

rules.title: Employer Liability for Intentional Torts
rules.prompt_question: When is an employer liable for an employee's intentional torts?
rules.rule_statement: An employer may be vicariously liable for an employee's intentional tort if the act was within the scope of employment, such as when force is job-related, the act was motivated by a purpose to serve the employer, or the conduct was foreseeable in the employment context.
rules.application_example: A bouncer uses excessive force while removing a patron from a club.
rules.common_trap: Purely personal assaults usually fall outside the scope unless the job makes such confrontations foreseeable.

rules.title: Independent Contractor Rule
rules.prompt_question: Is a principal generally liable for torts of an independent contractor?
rules.rule_statement: A principal is generally not vicariously liable for torts committed by an independent contractor because the principal lacks the right to control the manner and means of the contractor's work.
rules.application_example: A homeowner generally is not liable for a painter's negligence if the painter is an independent contractor.
rules.common_trap: There are important exceptions, such as inherently dangerous activities or nondelegable duties.

rules.title: Exceptions for Independent Contractors
rules.prompt_question: What are common exceptions to the general rule against liability for independent contractors?
rules.rule_statement: A principal may be liable for an independent contractor's torts when the principal is negligent in selection, when the work is inherently dangerous, when a nondelegable duty is involved, or when the principal retains substantial control over the work.
rules.application_example: A landowner may be liable for blasting operations performed by an independent contractor because blasting is inherently dangerous.
rules.common_trap: The exception is based on the nature of the activity or duty, not merely the existence of a contract.

rules.title: Principal's Direct Liability
rules.prompt_question: When is a principal directly liable for an agent's conduct?
rules.rule_statement: A principal may be directly liable for its own negligence in selecting, supervising, or retaining an agent, or for authorizing or ratifying wrongful conduct.
rules.application_example: An employer hires a driver with a known history of reckless driving and may be directly liable for negligent hiring.
rules.common_trap: Direct liability is separate from respondeat superior and can exist even when vicarious liability does not.

rules.title: Agent's Duty of Loyalty
rules.prompt_question: What is an agent's duty of loyalty?
rules.rule_statement: An agent owes the principal a duty of loyalty and must act solely for the principal's benefit in matters connected with the agency, avoiding self-dealing, usurpation of business opportunities, undisclosed conflicts of interest, and secret profits.
rules.application_example: An agent cannot secretly accept a kickback from a supplier while purchasing for the principal.
rules.common_trap: Full disclosure and informed consent by the principal may permit conduct that otherwise would breach the duty of loyalty.

rules.title: Agent's Duty of Care
rules.prompt_question: What duty of care does an agent owe?
rules.rule_statement: An agent owes the principal a duty to act with the care, competence, and diligence normally exercised by agents in similar circumstances.
rules.application_example: A real-estate agent must use reasonable diligence in presenting offers to the principal.
rules.common_trap: The standard may be heightened if the agent claims special skills or expertise.

rules.title: Agent's Duty of Obedience
rules.prompt_question: What is the agent's duty of obedience?
rules.rule_statement: An agent must act within the scope of actual authority and obey the principal's lawful instructions concerning the agency relationship.
rules.application_example: An agent instructed to buy no more than 500 units breaches the duty of obedience by ordering 1,000 units.
rules.common_trap: An agent need not obey unlawful directions.

rules.title: Principal's Duties to Agent
rules.prompt_question: What duties does a principal owe an agent?
rules.rule_statement: A principal generally owes the agent duties of compensation as agreed, reimbursement and indemnification for authorized expenses and liabilities, and cooperation in the agency relationship.
rules.application_example: A principal reimburses an agent for reasonable travel expenses incurred while carrying out authorized business.
rules.common_trap: An agent usually is not entitled to reimbursement for expenses resulting from the agent's own fault or unauthorized conduct.

rules.title: General Partnership Formation
rules.prompt_question: How is a general partnership formed?
rules.rule_statement: A general partnership is formed when two or more persons associate to carry on as co-owners a business for profit, whether or not they intend to form a partnership.
rules.application_example: Two friends open a catering business and share profits without filing any documents, creating a general partnership.
rules.common_trap: Intent to form a partnership is not required if the parties objectively act as co-owners of a for-profit business.

rules.title: Profit Sharing Presumption
rules.prompt_question: When does profit sharing create a presumption of partnership?
rules.rule_statement: A person who receives a share of partnership profits is presumed to be a partner unless the profits were received in payment of a debt, wages, rent, interest on a loan, or for certain other exceptions.
rules.application_example: A person who receives 30 percent of net profits from a business is presumed to be a partner absent an exception.
rules.common_trap: Gross receipts alone do not establish a partnership, and profit sharing may be explained by an exception.

rules.title: No Filing Requirement for General Partnership
rules.prompt_question: Must a general partnership file a certificate to be formed?
rules.rule_statement: A general partnership need not file any organizational document to exist; it can arise by operation of law from the parties' conduct.
rules.application_example: A consulting business run jointly by two people can be a partnership even though no partnership papers were filed.
rules.common_trap: Limited partnerships and LLPs do require compliance with statutory filing requirements.

rules.title: Partner Authority
rules.prompt_question: When can a partner bind the partnership?
rules.rule_statement: Each partner is an agent of the partnership for carrying on the partnership's business in the ordinary course, and an act of a partner for apparently carrying on the ordinary course of the partnership business binds the partnership unless the partner lacked authority and the third party knew or had notice of that lack of authority.
rules.application_example: A partner in a law firm hires ordinary office services, binding the partnership.
rules.common_trap: Acts outside the ordinary course usually require authorization by the other partners.

rules.title: Acts Outside the Ordinary Course
rules.prompt_question: How are partner acts outside the ordinary course treated?
rules.rule_statement: An act of a partner that is not apparently for carrying on the partnership business in the ordinary course binds the partnership only if the act was authorized by the other partners.
rules.application_example: A partner selling substantially all partnership assets without approval usually does not bind the partnership.
rules.common_trap: Ordinary-course analysis depends on the nature of the partnership's business.

rules.title: Partner Liability for Partnership Obligations
rules.prompt_question: What is a partner's liability for partnership obligations?
rules.rule_statement: In a general partnership, all partners are jointly and severally liable for all obligations of the partnership unless otherwise provided by law.
rules.application_example: A creditor of the partnership can sue one partner for the full amount of a partnership debt.
rules.common_trap: A partner who pays more than that partner's share may seek contribution from the other partners.

rules.title: Partnership Liability for Partner Torts
rules.prompt_question: When is the partnership liable for a partner's tort?
rules.rule_statement: A partnership is liable for loss or injury caused to a person by a partner's wrongful act or omission acting in the ordinary course of the partnership business or with authority of the partnership.
rules.application_example: A law firm may be liable for a partner's malpractice committed while representing a client.
rules.common_trap: The partnership's liability does not eliminate the individual tortfeasor's own liability.

rules.title: Partner Duty of Loyalty
rules.prompt_question: What is a partner's duty of loyalty?
rules.rule_statement: A partner owes the partnership and the other partners a duty of loyalty, which includes accounting for benefits derived from partnership business or use of partnership property, refraining from dealing with the partnership on behalf of an adverse interest, and refraining from competing with the partnership before dissolution.
rules.application_example: A partner cannot secretly divert a partnership client to the partner's own separate business.
rules.common_trap: The duty of loyalty can be modified in part by agreement, but not eliminated in a way manifestly unreasonable under many statutes.

rules.title: Partner Duty of Care
rules.prompt_question: What duty of care does a partner owe?
rules.rule_statement: A partner owes the partnership and the other partners a duty of care limited to refraining from grossly negligent or reckless conduct, intentional misconduct, or a knowing violation of law.
rules.application_example: A partner who knowingly violates environmental law while acting for the partnership breaches the duty of care.
rules.common_trap: The partnership duty of care is lower than ordinary negligence in many statutory formulations.

rules.title: Management Rights
rules.prompt_question: How are management rights allocated in a general partnership?
rules.rule_statement: Absent agreement otherwise, all partners have equal rights in the management and conduct of the partnership business, and ordinary matters are decided by a majority of the partners while matters outside the ordinary course generally require unanimous consent.
rules.application_example: Two of three partners can decide an ordinary vendor contract, but admitting a new partner requires unanimity unless agreed otherwise.
rules.common_trap: Voting rights are usually equal per partner, not proportional to capital contribution, absent agreement.

rules.title: Profits and Losses
rules.prompt_question: How are profits and losses shared absent agreement?
rules.rule_statement: Absent agreement otherwise, partners share profits equally and share losses in the same proportion as profits.
rules.application_example: If two partners have no agreement, they split both profits and losses 50-50 even if one contributed more capital.
rules.common_trap: Students often assume contributions determine shares, but the default rule is equal sharing.

rules.title: Indemnification and Reimbursement
rules.prompt_question: When is a partnership required to indemnify a partner?
rules.rule_statement: A partnership must indemnify a partner for liabilities incurred in the ordinary course of the partnership's business or for preservation of its business or property, and reimburse a partner for payments made and liabilities incurred in the ordinary course or for preservation of the business or property.
rules.application_example: A partner who pays a partnership debt out of personal funds is entitled to reimbursement from the partnership.
rules.common_trap: The right may be limited when the liability resulted from the partner's wrongful conduct.

rules.title: Transfer of Partnership Interest
rules.prompt_question: What may a partner transfer without dissolving the partnership?
rules.rule_statement: A partner may transfer the partner's transferable interest, which generally consists of the partner's share of profits, losses, and distributions, but a transferee does not become a partner or acquire management rights without consent of the other partners.
rules.application_example: A partner assigns the right to receive future distributions to a creditor, but the creditor does not become a voting partner.
rules.common_trap: Economic rights are transferable; governance rights are not automatically transferable.

rules.title: Partnership Property
rules.prompt_question: Who owns partnership property?
rules.rule_statement: Property acquired by a partnership is property of the partnership and not of the partners individually.
rules.application_example: Real estate purchased in the partnership's name belongs to the partnership, not directly to the individual partners.
rules.common_trap: A partner cannot use partnership property for purely personal purposes absent authorization.

rules.title: Dissociation
rules.prompt_question: What is dissociation?
rules.rule_statement: Dissociation is the change in the relationship among the partners caused by a partner's ceasing to be associated in carrying on the business. It may be rightful or wrongful and does not always cause dissolution.
rules.application_example: A partner withdraws from a continuing partnership, causing dissociation but not necessarily winding up.
rules.common_trap: Under modern statutes, dissociation and dissolution are distinct concepts.

rules.title: Wrongful Dissociation
rules.prompt_question: When is a partner's dissociation wrongful?
rules.rule_statement: A partner's dissociation is wrongful if it breaches an express term of the partnership agreement or occurs before expiration of a term or completion of an undertaking in certain ways, such as wrongful withdrawal or bankruptcy where treated as wrongful by statute or agreement.
rules.application_example: A partner leaves a partnership formed for a fixed five-year term after one year without justification.
rules.common_trap: Wrongful dissociation may expose the departing partner to damages.

rules.title: Dissolution and Winding Up
rules.prompt_question: What is dissolution, and what follows it?
rules.rule_statement: Dissolution is the event triggering the winding up of partnership business. After dissolution, the partnership continues only for the purpose of winding up, including completing unfinished business, collecting assets, paying creditors, and distributing the surplus.
rules.application_example: Upon dissolution, partners stop taking new business except as appropriate to wind up existing matters.
rules.common_trap: Dissolution is distinct from termination; termination occurs after winding up is completed.

rules.title: Events Causing Dissolution
rules.prompt_question: What events generally cause dissolution of a general partnership?
rules.rule_statement: Dissolution may occur by the partners' will, expiration of a term or completion of an undertaking, an event agreed upon in the partnership agreement, judicial decree, or in some cases certain partner dissociations in an at-will partnership.
rules.application_example: Any partner in an at-will partnership can generally trigger dissolution by express notice of withdrawal.
rules.common_trap: In a term partnership, dissociation may not automatically dissolve the firm under modern statutes unless statutory conditions are met.

rules.title: Liability After Dissolution
rules.prompt_question: Can a partnership be bound after dissolution?
rules.rule_statement: After dissolution, a partnership may be bound by acts appropriate for winding up or by acts that would have bound the partnership before dissolution if the third party lacked notice of the dissolution, subject to statutory filing and notice provisions.
rules.application_example: A former partner enters an ordinary contract with a vendor unaware that the partnership dissolved, and the partnership may still be bound.
rules.common_trap: Notice rules matter greatly in cutting off lingering apparent authority after dissolution.

rules.title: Limited Liability Partnership
rules.prompt_question: How is a limited liability partnership created and what is its key effect?
rules.rule_statement: A limited liability partnership is created by filing the required statement with the state, and its principal effect is that partners are generally not personally liable for obligations of the partnership solely by reason of being partners.
rules.application_example: A law firm registers as an LLP so that partners are generally shielded from personal liability for firm debts and malpractice of other partners.
rules.common_trap: A partner remains liable for the partner's own wrongful acts and often for personal guarantees.

rules.title: Limited Partnership Formation
rules.prompt_question: How is a limited partnership formed?
rules.rule_statement: A limited partnership is formed by filing a certificate of limited partnership or similar document with the appropriate state office, naming at least one general partner and one or more limited partners.
rules.application_example: Investors form a limited partnership for a real-estate fund by filing the required certificate.
rules.common_trap: Unlike a general partnership, a limited partnership does not arise merely from conduct without statutory filing.

rules.title: General Partner Liability in LP
rules.prompt_question: What is the liability of a general partner in a limited partnership?
rules.rule_statement: A general partner in a limited partnership generally has management rights and is personally liable for the obligations of the limited partnership, much like a partner in a general partnership, unless the general partner is itself an entity providing liability protection.
rules.application_example: The general partner of a limited partnership may be personally liable for an LP contract debt.
rules.common_trap: Many modern LPs use a corporation or LLC as the general partner to avoid personal liability.

rules.title: Limited Partner Liability
rules.prompt_question: When is a limited partner personally liable?
rules.rule_statement: A limited partner is generally not personally liable for the obligations of the limited partnership solely by reason of being a limited partner, even if the limited partner participates in management under modern statutes.
rules.application_example: A limited partner votes on major structural decisions but still retains limited liability under modern law.
rules.common_trap: Older cases emphasized a control limitation, but modern statutes generally eliminate personal liability solely for participating in control.

rules.title: Control Limitation
rules.prompt_question: What is the traditional control limitation for limited partners?
rules.rule_statement: Traditionally, a limited partner risked personal liability if the limited partner participated substantially in control of the business and a third party reasonably believed the limited partner was a general partner. Modern statutes usually abolish or greatly narrow this control-based liability rule.
rules.application_example: Under older law, a limited partner who actively managed operations could risk liability to third parties.
rules.common_trap: Always check whether the exam expects modern statutory treatment or recognizes the historical doctrine as part of the outline.

rules.title: Economic Rights of Limited Partners
rules.prompt_question: What economic rights do limited partners typically have?
rules.rule_statement: Limited partners generally have the right to share in profits, losses, and distributions as provided by the partnership agreement, and they may transfer economic interests subject to agreement and statute.
rules.application_example: A limited partner is entitled to periodic distributions according to the LP agreement.
rules.common_trap: Economic rights do not necessarily include management or information rights beyond those granted by statute or agreement.

rules.title: Disclosure Requirements
rules.prompt_question: Why are disclosure requirements important for limited partnerships?
rules.rule_statement: A limited partnership must generally comply with statutory naming and filing requirements so that third parties can determine the entity's existence and identify persons with management authority.
rules.application_example: The public filing identifies the LP and its registered agent, helping third parties verify the entity.
rules.common_trap: Failure to comply may impair entity status or create problems with authority and notice.

rules.title: Corporate Formation
rules.prompt_question: How is a corporation formed?
rules.rule_statement: A corporation is formed by filing articles of incorporation with the state. Upon filing or upon the effective date stated in the articles, the corporation comes into existence as a separate legal entity.
rules.application_example: Entrepreneurs file articles with the secretary of state to create a corporation.
rules.common_trap: Promoter activity before formation is governed by pre-incorporation rules, not ordinary corporate rules.

rules.title: Articles of Incorporation
rules.prompt_question: What information is typically required in articles of incorporation?
rules.rule_statement: Articles of incorporation generally must include the corporate name, the number of authorized shares, the street address of the corporation's initial registered office and registered agent, and the name and address of each incorporator, along with any optional provisions.
rules.application_example: The articles list the corporation's name, registered agent, and share structure.
rules.common_trap: Specific state statutes may require additional or slightly different information.

rules.title: Bylaws
rules.prompt_question: What are bylaws?
rules.rule_statement: Bylaws are internal rules for managing the corporation and regulating its affairs. They are typically adopted by the incorporators or directors and may later be amended by shareholders or directors as allowed by statute and the articles.
rules.application_example: Bylaws may specify meeting procedures and officer positions.
rules.common_trap: Bylaws do not have to be filed with the state and cannot conflict with the articles or governing statute.

rules.title: De Facto Corporation
rules.prompt_question: What is a de facto corporation?
rules.rule_statement: A de facto corporation may exist when there is a valid incorporation statute, a good-faith colorable attempt to comply with it, and actual use of the corporate form, protecting participants from personal liability in some jurisdictions.
rules.application_example: Founders mistakenly file defective incorporation papers but begin doing business as a corporation.
rules.common_trap: Many modern statutes limit or abolish de facto corporation doctrine, so exam treatment may depend on the jurisdiction or outline expectations.

rules.title: Corporation by Estoppel
rules.prompt_question: What is corporation by estoppel?
rules.rule_statement: Under corporation by estoppel, a person who treated an enterprise as a corporation in dealing with it may be estopped from later denying its corporate existence to impose personal liability on the organizers.
rules.application_example: A vendor contracts with 'XYZ, Inc.' and may be estopped from suing the shareholders personally by denying the corporation existed.
rules.common_trap: This doctrine is equitable and fact-specific, and many modern statutes reduce its practical importance.

rules.title: Promoter Liability on Preincorporation Contracts
rules.prompt_question: Is a promoter liable on a preincorporation contract?
rules.rule_statement: A promoter who enters a contract on behalf of a not-yet-formed corporation is personally liable on the contract unless the contract clearly provides otherwise or there is a later novation releasing the promoter.
rules.application_example: A promoter signs a lease for future corporate office space before the corporation is formed and remains liable absent novation.
rules.common_trap: Corporate adoption of the contract alone does not automatically release the promoter.

rules.title: Corporate Adoption of Preincorporation Contracts
rules.prompt_question: How may a corporation become bound on a preincorporation contract?
rules.rule_statement: After formation, a corporation may adopt a preincorporation contract expressly or impliedly, making the corporation liable on the contract, though the promoter remains liable unless there is a novation.
rules.application_example: A newly formed corporation begins operating from leased premises and pays rent, implying adoption of the promoter's lease contract.
rules.common_trap: Adoption binds the corporation but does not itself discharge the promoter.

rules.title: Promoter Fiduciary Duties
rules.prompt_question: What fiduciary duties does a promoter owe?
rules.rule_statement: A promoter owes fiduciary duties of good faith and fair dealing to the corporation and its investors, including the duty to disclose material facts and avoid secret profits from transactions involving the corporation.
rules.application_example: A promoter selling property to the corporation at a markup must disclose the profit to avoid liability.
rules.common_trap: The extent of disclosure required may depend on whether disclosure is made to all subscribers, directors, or an independent board.

rules.title: Subscriptions for Shares
rules.prompt_question: What is a subscription for shares before incorporation?
rules.rule_statement: A preincorporation subscription is an offer to buy stock in the future corporation and is often irrevocable for the period specified by statute unless all subscribers agree to revoke.
rules.application_example: An investor signs a subscription agreement to purchase 1,000 shares once the corporation is formed.
rules.common_trap: Modern statutes often make preincorporation subscriptions irrevocable for a limited period, commonly six months, unless otherwise stated.

rules.title: Piercing the Corporate Veil
rules.prompt_question: When may a court pierce the corporate veil?
rules.rule_statement: A court may pierce the corporate veil and hold shareholders personally liable when the corporation is used to perpetrate fraud, is inadequately capitalized, or is treated as the shareholders' alter ego, and failing to pierce would sanction unfairness or injustice.
rules.application_example: A shareholder strips a corporation of assets and uses it as a shell to avoid creditors.
rules.common_trap: Courts are more reluctant to pierce in contract cases where creditors voluntarily dealt with the corporation.

rules.title: Common Veil-Piercing Factors
rules.prompt_question: What factors do courts commonly consider in veil-piercing analysis?
rules.rule_statement: Common veil-piercing factors include undercapitalization, failure to observe corporate formalities, commingling of assets, treating corporate assets as personal assets, overlapping ownership and control, and using the corporation to promote fraud or injustice.
rules.application_example: A sole shareholder pays personal bills from the corporate account and never holds meetings or keeps records.
rules.common_trap: No single factor is determinative; courts look at the totality of the circumstances.

rules.title: Reverse Piercing Concept
rules.prompt_question: What is reverse piercing?
rules.rule_statement: Reverse piercing is the theory that a claimant may seek to reach corporate assets for the liabilities of a shareholder, though courts treat it cautiously because it may prejudice innocent shareholders or creditors.
rules.application_example: A creditor of a dominant shareholder tries to reach the shareholder's corporation's assets.
rules.common_trap: Traditional veil piercing is far more commonly tested than reverse piercing.

rules.title: Board Management Authority
rules.prompt_question: Who manages the corporation?
rules.rule_statement: The corporation's business and affairs are managed by or under the direction of the board of directors, except as otherwise provided by statute, the articles, or valid shareholder arrangements in special contexts.
rules.application_example: The board decides whether to enter a major supply agreement.
rules.common_trap: Shareholders own the corporation but generally do not manage day-to-day affairs.

rules.title: Board Meetings, Notice, and Quorum
rules.prompt_question: What are the basic rules for board meetings?
rules.rule_statement: A majority of the number of directors fixed by the articles or bylaws generally constitutes a quorum for board action, and directors typically must receive notice of special meetings unless waived. No notice is usually required for regular meetings if the time and place are fixed.
rules.application_example: A five-member board generally needs three directors for a quorum unless the governing documents provide otherwise within statutory limits.
rules.common_trap: Board action requires both a quorum and the required vote, which is usually a majority of directors present unless a greater vote is required.

rules.title: Board Action by Written Consent
rules.prompt_question: Can directors act without a meeting?
rules.rule_statement: Directors may act without a meeting if all directors consent in writing or in an electronic record, unless the governing statute provides otherwise.
rules.application_example: All directors sign a unanimous written consent approving a merger agreement.
rules.common_trap: Unlike shareholder voting, director written consent is often required to be unanimous unless a statute says otherwise.

rules.title: Board Committees
rules.prompt_question: What may a board committee do?
rules.rule_statement: The board may delegate many management functions to committees of directors, but certain fundamental decisions, such as declaring distributions or approving major structural changes, may remain reserved to the full board under statute.
rules.application_example: An audit committee oversees financial reporting, while the full board approves major mergers.
rules.common_trap: The exact list of nondelegable matters is statutory and often tested conceptually.

rules.title: Director's Objection
rules.prompt_question: How may a director avoid being deemed to have assented to board action?
rules.rule_statement: A director who is present at a meeting when corporate action is taken is generally deemed to have assented unless the director objects at the beginning of the meeting or promptly upon arrival to holding it or transacting business, or the director votes against or abstains and the abstention or dissent is recorded.
rules.application_example: A director votes against an unlawful dividend and has the dissent recorded in the minutes.
rules.common_trap: Silence at the meeting may be treated as assent.

rules.title: Officer Authority
rules.prompt_question: How do officers obtain authority to bind the corporation?
rules.rule_statement: Corporate officers have actual authority as granted by the bylaws, board resolutions, or their office, and may also have apparent authority to bind the corporation in matters ordinary for their position.
rules.application_example: A corporate president usually has apparent authority to enter ordinary business contracts.
rules.common_trap: An officer's title does not always confer authority for extraordinary transactions such as selling all corporate assets.

rules.title: Officer Liability on Corporate Obligations
rules.prompt_question: When is an officer personally liable on a corporate contract?
rules.rule_statement: An officer who acts for a disclosed corporation is generally not personally liable on a corporate contract if the officer acts with authority and indicates representative capacity, unless the officer personally guarantees the obligation or lacks authority.
rules.application_example: A corporate treasurer signs a loan agreement on behalf of the corporation and is not personally liable absent a guarantee.
rules.common_trap: Signing only one's own name without indicating agency status may create ambiguity and personal liability issues.

rules.title: Shareholder Meetings
rules.prompt_question: What are the basic rules for annual and special shareholder meetings?
rules.rule_statement: Corporations generally must hold annual shareholder meetings, and special meetings may be called by persons authorized by statute, the articles, or bylaws. Notice must state the time and place and, for special meetings, often the purpose.
rules.application_example: The board calls the annual shareholder meeting to elect directors.
rules.common_trap: Action taken at a special meeting outside the noticed purpose may be invalid in some circumstances.

rules.title: Shareholder Quorum and Voting
rules.prompt_question: What are the default quorum and voting rules for shareholders?
rules.rule_statement: A majority of the votes entitled to be cast on a matter generally constitutes a quorum for shareholder action, and if a quorum exists, action is approved if the votes cast in favor exceed votes cast against, unless the articles, bylaws, or statute require a greater vote.
keywords: majority of votes entitled to be cast
rules.application_example: If holders of a majority of shares are present, the shareholders may vote on routine matters at the meeting.
rules.common_trap: Quorum and voting thresholds are distinct; once quorum is present, the required affirmative vote may be less than a majority of outstanding shares.

rules.title: Proxy Voting
rules.prompt_question: What is a proxy?
rules.rule_statement: A proxy is an authorization by a shareholder permitting another person to vote the shareholder's shares. Proxies are generally freely revocable unless coupled with an interest and stated to be irrevocable.
rules.application_example: A shareholder signs a proxy authorizing another person to vote at the annual meeting.
rules.common_trap: Voting trusts and proxy arrangements are distinct concepts.

rules.title: Voting Trusts
rules.prompt_question: What is a voting trust?
rules.rule_statement: A voting trust is an arrangement by which shareholders transfer legal title of their shares to a trustee who votes the shares pursuant to the trust agreement, while the beneficial owners retain economic interests.
rules.application_example: Founders place their shares into a voting trust to ensure unified control over corporate elections.
rules.common_trap: Voting trusts typically require formalities such as a written agreement and share transfer.

rules.title: Shareholder Voting Agreements
rules.prompt_question: Are shareholder voting agreements valid?
rules.rule_statement: Shareholder voting agreements are generally valid and specifically enforceable if they do not violate statute or public policy.
rules.application_example: Two shareholders agree in writing to vote together for each other's nominees.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse a voting agreement with a voting trust; the former usually does not require share transfer.

rules.title: Cumulative Voting
rules.prompt_question: What is cumulative voting?
rules.rule_statement: Cumulative voting allows shareholders in director elections to multiply the number of shares owned by the number of directors to be elected and cast the resulting votes for one or more candidates, if authorized by the corporation's governing law or charter.
rules.application_example: A minority shareholder pools votes to elect at least one board member under cumulative voting.
rules.common_trap: Cumulative voting is not universal; many corporations do not provide it absent charter authorization or statute.

rules.title: Class Voting
rules.prompt_question: When is class voting required?
rules.rule_statement: Certain corporate actions that affect a class of shares differently, such as amendments altering class rights, may require separate class voting by that class in addition to any overall shareholder vote.
rules.application_example: An amendment reducing dividend rights of preferred stock may require approval by the preferred class.
rules.common_trap: Class voting focuses on direct legal effect on the class, not merely economic consequences shared by all shareholders.

rules.subtopic: Close Corporations and Special Control Devices
rules.title: Close Corporation Agreements
rules.prompt_question: What special agreements are common in close corporations?
rules.rule_statement: Close corporation shareholders may adopt agreements restricting share transfer, allocating management powers among shareholders, resolving deadlocks, or creating buy-sell rights, and such agreements are often enforceable if consistent with statute.
rules.application_example: A close corporation agreement gives shareholders the right of first refusal before any share sale to outsiders.
rules.common_trap: Because close corporations resemble partnerships, courts may scrutinize oppression and fiduciary issues closely.

rules.title: Director Duty of Care
rules.prompt_question: What is the director's duty of care?
rules.rule_statement: A director must act in good faith, with the care that a person in a like position would reasonably believe appropriate under similar circumstances, and in a manner the director reasonably believes to be in the best interests of the corporation.
rules.application_example: Directors reviewing a merger should inform themselves and deliberate before approving the transaction.
rules.common_trap: Courts generally do not second-guess informed, disinterested business decisions because of the business judgment rule.

rules.title: Business Judgment Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the business judgment rule?
rules.rule_statement: The business judgment rule creates a presumption that directors acted on an informed basis, in good faith, and with the honest belief that the action taken was in the corporation's best interests. Courts generally will not second-guess such decisions absent fraud, illegality, conflict of interest, or gross abuse.
rules.application_example: A board's good-faith decision to expand into a new market is usually protected even if the venture later fails.
rules.common_trap: The rule protects process and good-faith business judgment, not self-dealing or uninformed decisions.

rules.title: Director Duty of Loyalty
rules.prompt_question: What is the director's duty of loyalty?
rules.rule_statement: A director owes the corporation a duty of loyalty and must not engage in self-dealing, usurp corporate opportunities, compete unfairly with the corporation, or otherwise place personal interests above the corporation's interests.
rules.application_example: A director cannot secretly acquire for personal benefit a business opportunity the corporation was considering.
rules.common_trap: Duty-of-loyalty violations are not protected by the business judgment rule.

rules.title: Director Conflict-of-Interest Transaction
rules.prompt_question: When is a director conflict-of-interest transaction valid?
rules.rule_statement: A director's conflicting-interest transaction is generally not automatically void if it is disclosed and approved by disinterested directors or shareholders, or if the transaction was fair to the corporation at the time it was entered.
rules.application_example: A corporation leases office space from a director after full disclosure and approval by disinterested directors.
rules.common_trap: Approval procedures and fairness both matter; disclosure alone is not always enough if the statute requires proper approval.

rules.title: Corporate Opportunity Doctrine
rules.prompt_question: What is the corporate opportunity doctrine?
rules.rule_statement: Directors and officers may not take for themselves a business opportunity that is within the corporation's line of business, that the corporation has an interest or expectancy in, or that the corporation is financially able to undertake, unless the opportunity is first offered to the corporation and properly rejected.
rules.application_example: A director learns of land ideal for corporate expansion and may not buy it personally without first offering it to the corporation.
rules.common_trap: Disclosure and rejection by disinterested decision-makers may cleanse the transaction.

rules.title: Officer Fiduciary Duties
rules.prompt_question: Do officers owe fiduciary duties?
rules.rule_statement: Corporate officers owe fiduciary duties of care and loyalty similar to those owed by directors, adapted to their role and authority within the corporation.
rules.application_example: A chief financial officer may be liable for self-dealing or grossly negligent financial oversight.
rules.common_trap: Because officers act operationally, agency authority issues often overlap with fiduciary-duty issues.

rules.title: Controlling Shareholder Duties
rules.prompt_question: When may a controlling shareholder owe fiduciary duties?
rules.rule_statement: A controlling shareholder may owe fiduciary duties to minority shareholders and the corporation when exercising control, particularly in freeze-outs, self-dealing transactions, or oppressive conduct in close corporations.
rules.application_example: A majority shareholder causes the corporation to enter a transaction benefiting the majority at the minority's expense.
rules.common_trap: Ordinary voting in one's own interest is usually allowed; the problem arises when control is used oppressively or self-interestedly.

rules.subtopic: Close Corporations and Special Control Devices
rules.title: Minority Oppression in Close Corporations
rules.prompt_question: What is minority oppression in a close corporation?
rules.rule_statement: Minority oppression occurs when controlling shareholders in a close corporation frustrate the reasonable expectations of minority owners through exclusion from management, withholding distributions, termination of employment, or other unfair conduct. Courts may grant equitable relief, including buyouts or dissolution.
rules.application_example: Majority shareholders freeze out a minority shareholder by firing the shareholder from the family business and stopping all dividends.
rules.common_trap: Close corporation disputes are often analyzed more like partnership disputes than public-company disputes.

rules.title: Distributions to Shareholders
rules.prompt_question: When may a corporation make distributions?
rules.rule_statement: A corporation may make distributions to shareholders unless, after giving effect to the distribution, the corporation would be unable to pay its debts as they become due in the usual course of business or its total assets would be less than the sum of its total liabilities and any preferential amounts due to superior shares on dissolution.
keywords: unable to pay debts as they become due
rules.application_example: A board may declare a dividend only if the corporation remains solvent under statutory tests.
rules.common_trap: The exact statutory formulation varies, but solvency-based limits are central.

rules.title: Director Liability for Unlawful Distribution
rules.prompt_question: When are directors liable for unlawful distributions?
rules.rule_statement: Directors who vote for or assent to an unlawful distribution may be personally liable to the corporation for the amount exceeding what could have been properly distributed if they failed to comply with the applicable standard of conduct.
rules.application_example: Directors approve a dividend that leaves the corporation insolvent and may be liable for resulting loss.
rules.common_trap: A director may seek contribution from other liable directors and sometimes from shareholders who knowingly received improper distributions.

rules.title: Authorized Shares and Issuance
rules.prompt_question: What is the significance of authorized shares?
rules.rule_statement: The articles fix the number of shares the corporation is authorized to issue. The board may issue shares up to that number in exchange for consideration approved by the board, subject to statutory requirements.
rules.application_example: A corporation authorized for one million shares may issue only up to that amount unless the articles are amended.
rules.common_trap: Issuing shares beyond the authorized number creates overissue problems.

rules.title: Preemptive Rights
rules.prompt_question: What are preemptive rights?
rules.rule_statement: Preemptive rights give existing shareholders the right to maintain their proportional ownership by purchasing a proportionate share of certain new issuances, but such rights generally exist only if granted by the articles or governing statute.
rules.application_example: A charter grants shareholders the right to buy a pro rata portion of newly issued common stock.
rules.common_trap: Modern statutes often do not grant preemptive rights by default.

rules.title: Treasury Shares
rules.prompt_question: What are treasury shares?
rules.rule_statement: Treasury shares are shares previously issued and later reacquired by the corporation. They are generally treated as authorized but unissued shares unless the articles provide otherwise.
rules.application_example: A corporation buys back some of its shares and later reissues them as treasury shares.
rules.common_trap: Treasury shares usually do not carry voting or dividend rights while held by the corporation.

rules.title: Inspection Rights
rules.prompt_question: What records may shareholders inspect?
rules.rule_statement: Shareholders generally have the right to inspect certain corporate records, such as articles, bylaws, minutes, and accounting records, if they comply with statutory procedures and, for some records, show a proper purpose.
rules.application_example: A shareholder seeks inspection of accounting records to investigate suspected mismanagement.
rules.common_trap: Basic records may be inspectable more freely than sensitive records requiring a proper purpose.

rules.title: Direct Action
rules.prompt_question: When may a shareholder bring a direct action?
rules.rule_statement: A shareholder may bring a direct action for injuries suffered individually, such as denial of voting rights, denial of inspection rights, or oppression, rather than injuries suffered by the corporation as a whole.
rules.application_example: A shareholder sues directly after being denied the right to vote shares at a meeting.
rules.common_trap: If the injury is primarily to the corporation, the proper action is derivative rather than direct.

rules.title: Derivative Action
rules.prompt_question: What is a derivative action?
rules.rule_statement: A derivative action is a suit brought by a shareholder on behalf of the corporation to enforce a corporate cause of action when those in control of the corporation fail to do so.
rules.application_example: A shareholder sues directors derivatively for waste of corporate assets.
rules.common_trap: Any recovery generally goes to the corporation, not directly to the shareholder-plaintiff.

rules.title: Derivative Action Requirements
rules.prompt_question: What must a shareholder generally show to bring a derivative suit?
rules.rule_statement: A derivative plaintiff generally must have been a shareholder at the time of the challenged act or have acquired shares by operation of law, must fairly and adequately represent the corporation's interests, and must make a written demand on the corporation unless demand is excused where recognized by law.
rules.application_example: A current shareholder who owned stock when the alleged self-dealing occurred makes a written demand on the board before suing.
rules.common_trap: Demand requirements and whether demand futility is recognized vary by statute and jurisdiction.

rules.title: Demand Requirement
rules.prompt_question: What is the demand requirement in derivative litigation?
rules.rule_statement: Before filing a derivative action, a shareholder usually must make a written demand requesting that the corporation bring the action, and then wait the required statutory period unless irreparable injury would result or demand is excused under applicable law.
rules.application_example: A shareholder sends a formal demand letter asking the board to sue a director for self-dealing.
rules.common_trap: Some modern statutes require demand in all cases, while others allow demand futility exceptions.

rules.title: Special Litigation Committee
rules.prompt_question: What is a special litigation committee?
rules.rule_statement: A corporation may appoint a special litigation committee of disinterested directors or others authorized by statute to investigate whether pursuing a derivative claim is in the corporation's best interests, and its recommendation may support dismissal if statutory standards are satisfied.
rules.application_example: A committee of independent directors investigates a derivative suit and recommends dismissal as not in the corporation's best interests.
rules.common_trap: Courts scrutinize committee independence and investigative good faith.

rules.title: Shareholder Class Actions
rules.prompt_question: When might shareholders proceed by class action instead of derivative suit?
rules.rule_statement: A shareholder class action may be appropriate when shareholders suffer a common direct injury, such as misleading proxy disclosures affecting voting or transactions impacting shareholders in the same direct manner.
rules.application_example: Shareholders sue as a class for inadequate disclosures in a merger vote.
rules.common_trap: Derivative claims belong to the corporation and are not transformed into class claims merely because many shareholders are affected indirectly.

rules.title: LLC Formation
rules.prompt_question: How is an LLC formed?
rules.rule_statement: A limited liability company is formed by filing articles of organization or a certificate of formation with the state, creating a separate legal entity distinct from its members.
rules.application_example: Entrepreneurs file a certificate of formation to create an LLC for a startup venture.
rules.common_trap: An operating agreement is important but filing creates the entity.

rules.title: Operating Agreement
rules.prompt_question: What is an LLC operating agreement?
rules.rule_statement: An operating agreement governs the relations among the members and managers and between them and the LLC, including management structure, allocations, distributions, transfer restrictions, and dissolution rights, subject to nonwaivable statutory limits.
rules.application_example: An operating agreement provides that one member manages daily operations while profits are split 60-40.
rules.common_trap: Some fiduciary duties may be modified by the operating agreement within statutory limits, but not all duties can be eliminated.

rules.title: Member-Managed LLC
rules.prompt_question: What is a member-managed LLC?
rules.rule_statement: In a member-managed LLC, each member has authority to participate in management, and each member is generally an agent of the LLC for matters in the ordinary course of the LLC's activities.
rules.application_example: In a small LLC with no managers, each member can bind the LLC in ordinary transactions.
rules.common_trap: Always check whether the operating agreement instead creates a manager-managed LLC.

rules.title: Manager-Managed LLC
rules.prompt_question: What is a manager-managed LLC?
rules.rule_statement: In a manager-managed LLC, management authority is vested in one or more managers rather than the members, and ordinary management powers belong to the managers, while members usually act more like passive owners unless the operating agreement provides otherwise.
rules.application_example: An LLC appoints one professional manager to run operations while outside investors remain members only.
rules.common_trap: Members in a manager-managed LLC generally lack ordinary agency authority solely by being members.

rules.title: Member Liability
rules.prompt_question: Are LLC members personally liable for LLC obligations?
rules.rule_statement: LLC members are generally not personally liable for the debts, obligations, or liabilities of the LLC solely by reason of being members or participating in management.
rules.application_example: A creditor of the LLC ordinarily cannot collect the LLC's contract debt from a member personally.
rules.common_trap: Members remain liable for their own torts, personal guarantees, or veil-piercing circumstances.

rules.title: Manager Authority
rules.prompt_question: How do managers bind the LLC?
rules.rule_statement: Managers in a manager-managed LLC have actual and often apparent authority to bind the LLC in the ordinary course of the LLC's business, subject to limitations in the operating agreement and statutory notice rules.
rules.application_example: An LLC manager signs a routine vendor agreement for the company.
rules.common_trap: Extraordinary transactions may require member approval under the operating agreement or statute.

rules.title: LLC Fiduciary Duties
rules.prompt_question: What fiduciary duties may members and managers owe in an LLC?
rules.rule_statement: Depending on the statute and operating agreement, members in a member-managed LLC and managers in a manager-managed LLC generally owe duties of loyalty and care to the LLC and, in some circumstances, to the other members, though the operating agreement may modify these duties within statutory bounds.
rules.application_example: A managing member may not divert LLC business opportunities for personal gain without disclosure and consent.
rules.common_trap: LLC fiduciary rules are often more contractarian than corporate rules, so the operating agreement matters greatly.

rules.title: Transfer of LLC Interests
rules.prompt_question: What may an LLC member transfer?
rules.rule_statement: Absent agreement otherwise, a member may transfer the member's transferable interest, which generally consists of economic rights to distributions, but the transferee does not become a member or obtain management rights without the consent required by statute or the operating agreement.
rules.application_example: A member assigns the right to receive future LLC distributions to a creditor, but the creditor cannot vote or manage the LLC.
rules.common_trap: This resembles partnership transferability rules more than freely transferable corporate stock.

rules.title: LLC Dissociation and Dissolution
rules.prompt_question: How do dissociation and dissolution work in an LLC?
rules.rule_statement: A member may dissociate under the statute or operating agreement, and dissolution may occur upon events specified in the operating agreement, member consent, expiration of duration, judicial decree, or other statutory grounds, followed by winding up of LLC affairs.
rules.application_example: Members vote to dissolve the LLC and liquidate its remaining assets.
rules.common_trap: Modern LLC statutes often allow the entity to continue despite a member's dissociation.

rules.title: Mergers and Consolidations
rules.prompt_question: What approvals are generally required for a merger?
rules.rule_statement: A merger generally requires board approval and shareholder approval by the requisite vote of each constituent corporation, unless a statutory short-form merger or other exception applies. The surviving entity succeeds to the rights and liabilities of the constituents by operation of law.
rules.application_example: Two corporations merge after both boards approve and their shareholders vote in favor.
rules.common_trap: Appraisal rights may arise for dissenting shareholders in certain mergers.

rules.title: Sale of Substantially All Assets
rules.prompt_question: What approvals are generally required for a sale of substantially all assets?
rules.rule_statement: A sale, lease, exchange, or other disposition of all or substantially all corporate assets outside the usual and regular course of business generally requires board approval and shareholder approval, while ordinary-course dispositions usually do not.
rules.application_example: A manufacturing company sells its entire operating division, requiring shareholder approval.
rules.common_trap: The test is both quantitative and qualitative in many jurisdictions.

rules.title: Amending Articles
rules.prompt_question: How are articles of incorporation generally amended?
rules.rule_statement: An amendment to the articles of incorporation generally requires board adoption and shareholder approval, plus filing of articles of amendment with the state, unless a statutory exception permits board-only action.
rules.application_example: A corporation amends its articles to increase authorized shares after board and shareholder approval.
rules.common_trap: Certain class-voting rights may be triggered by the amendment.

rules.title: Corporate Dissolution
rules.prompt_question: How is a corporation voluntarily dissolved?
rules.rule_statement: A corporation may generally dissolve voluntarily by board action followed by shareholder approval and filing articles of dissolution, after which the corporation continues only to wind up affairs, pay creditors, and distribute remaining assets.
rules.application_example: A small corporation ceases operations, files dissolution papers, pays creditors, and distributes remaining cash to shareholders.
rules.common_trap: Dissolution does not instantly terminate all corporate existence; winding up continues the entity for limited purposes.

rules.title: Judicial Dissolution
rules.prompt_question: When may a court order dissolution?
rules.rule_statement: A court may order dissolution in cases such as deadlock, waste, fraud, illegality, or oppression of minority owners, depending on the entity type and statute.
rules.application_example: Shareholders in a two-owner close corporation are deadlocked and the court orders dissolution.
rules.common_trap: Courts may grant less drastic relief, such as buyout or appointment of a custodian, when authorized by statute.

rules.title: Parent-Subsidiary Structure
rules.prompt_question: What is the basic legal relationship between a parent and subsidiary?
rules.rule_statement: A parent corporation that owns a controlling interest in a subsidiary does not automatically become liable for the subsidiary's obligations merely by ownership; each corporation remains a separate legal entity absent veil-piercing, assumption of liability, or another exception.
rules.application_example: A parent company generally is not liable for a subsidiary's contract debts solely because it owns the subsidiary's shares.
rules.common_trap: Operational control plus misuse of the corporate form may support veil-piercing in appropriate cases.
